I turn complex technology into stories people actually understand.

Most companies don't have a product problem. They have a storytelling problem.

I work somewhere between product, sales, and reality. Most of the time, those three things are not saying the same thing. I help make them line up. Not with more slides. There are already enough slides.

The Problem I Think About

A lot of teams aren't struggling because the product is weak.

They're struggling because nobody can explain it.

What that usually looks like:

  • Sales leans on features because the story isn't clear
  • Messaging changes depending on who you talk to
  • Enablement turns into information overload
  • Buyers don't connect the product to their actual problem

The gap between what a product does and how it's understood? That's where things stall. That's the gap I tend to focus on.

20+
Years in Tech Evangelism, Enablement, & General Shenanigans
∞
Moments Where a Story Changed the Room

How I Think

Six things I keep coming back to.

01
Enablement isn't training. It's translation.

Training tells people what to do. Translation helps them understand why it matters — and how to carry it into any room they walk into.

02
If sales can't explain it, it doesn't exist.

It doesn't matter how good the product is if the person selling it can't make it land. Clarity isn't a nice-to-have. It's the whole game.

03
Features don't lose deals. Confusion does.

Most lost deals aren't lost on specs. They're lost when buyers couldn't connect the product to their actual problem. That's a story problem, not a product problem.

04
Story isn't a layer on top of the product.

It's how the product is understood. The narrative isn't something you add at the end of the roadmap — it's what makes everything else make sense.

05
Simplicity isn't dumbed down. It's earned.

The clearest explanations come from the deepest understanding. If you can't say it simply, you probably haven't figured it out yet.

06
If it only works in a slide, it probably doesn't work.

Real understanding travels. It shows up in conversations, in customer interactions, in how people describe the problem without a deck in front of them.

Currently

What I'm actually working on right now.

This isn't a highlight reel. It's the actual work — the things I'm building, hosting, and thinking through at the moment.

Internal Talk Show · BigID

D'Art & The Deal

A recurring internal show where I sit down with AEs right after they close a deal. We break down what actually happened — the conversation that shifted things, the moment the story landed. Real wins, in their own words.

Event Host · BigID

2026 CKO & SKO

Hosting BigID's Customer Kickoff and Sales Kickoff for 2026. The job isn't just to keep things moving — it's to hold the narrative thread across two days so the whole event lands as one coherent story.

Ongoing Work

Enablement Programs & Storytelling Frameworks

Building the infrastructure that helps teams carry the story without needing a slide deck to do it. Frameworks that travel. Programs that actually change how people talk about what they do.

Proof

Work that moved something.

Onboarding

Rebuilt onboarding around story

The situation: New hires could learn the product, but struggled to explain it.

The move: Shifted onboarding from product-first to story-first. If you can't explain it, you don't understand it.

Faster ramp · More consistent messaging · Fewer "what do we actually do?" moments
Positioning

Closed the story gap

The situation: Strong product. Inconsistent story. Sales was filling in the blanks on their own.

The move: Built a simple, repeatable narrative: what's the problem, what's changing, why it matters — then pressure-tested it with the field.

Clearer positioning · Better conversations · Less feature dumping
Enablement

Made enablement more useful

The situation: People attended sessions, but didn't retain much.

The move: Shifted to a more conversational format. Real people, real examples, practical takeaways.

Higher engagement · Stronger recall · More ideas actually getting used
Knowledge

Turned scattered knowledge into something usable

The situation: Good ideas were stuck in conversations and didn't travel.

The move: Captured and structured them into simple, repeatable frameworks.

Less reinvention · More consistency · Better alignment across teams
